comments.text:
morning beach walk…
millions of years tumble
into my hand #petoskeystone #michigan #dailyhaikuprompt #nature
#sensoryart #haiku A Petoskey stone is a fossil colonial coral. These corals lived in warm shallow seas that covered Michigan during Devonian time, some 350 million years ago (Michigan.gov)
I found a heart upon the stones, I wondered if it had been carelessly discarded or if it had finally made its way home❤️. #RockinTuesday #naturephotography #lakemichigan

Rock hounds advise searching for Petoskey stones near Lake Michigan. Go while it's raining if you want to make them easier to spot, because the water fills in tiny cracks that scatter light, making the coral's hexagonal structure more apparent.

Some rock tumbler photos. This is a batch that’s moving from week two to week three. Upper LH: chevron amethyst, upper RH random jaspers and a piece of opelina, lower RH all stones from Lake Michigan, lower LH tree agate.
What none of you guys know is that Port Puddingstone is only as nice as it is thanks to an Omelas-esque situation where a Silicon Valley techbro is entombed beneath the bed of Lake Huron.

An amazing headband made of dyed porcupine quills by an Indigenous person in the Great Lakes region around 1760. It is in such remarkable condition because it was kept in a Scottish castle for 250 years.

Amazing Petoskey stone found at Lake Huron. Petoskey stones are rocks and fossils at the same time, composed of fossilized coral. More about Petoskey stones and more beautiful examples👇
earthlymission.com/petoskey-sto... 📷 pierogi4life

This is Yooperlite or fluorescent sodalite. It’s only found in Michigan typically on the shores of Lake Superior. This gray stone illuminates under a black light. It never ceases to amaze me what the Earth creates (and even cooler that this stone is found solely in the US.)

For this week’s #water prompt, I chose to print a Petoskey stone. These “stones” are actually 350 million year old fossilized coral found on the shores of the Great Lakes in Michigan’s lower peninsula. #PrintPromptMonday #printmaking #linocut #PureMichigan

Found a crystalized, fossilized piece of horn coral whilst at the beach near the great lakes, been waltzing all over the place and getting more encounters in for Vulpix :) lmk if you want to see more of my crystal and rock collection! #crystals #fossils #rockhounding
Petoskey stones found around Lake Michigan look very much like these. I have a couple of small pebble-size ones which I treasure. The patterns are fossilized coral.
Fossils from Lake Michigan near Petoskey. Lots of cool bits including corals, crinoids, and what I think are brachiopods and maybe a trilobite?
Found this fossil stone recently along Lake Ontario. Believe it is Cladopora, a branching coral found in Devonian limestone, about 400m yrs old. Saw a few others, this was the largest one.

It's from the Chazy Formation, the oldest strata in the Great Lakes-St. Lawrence Valley that has well-preserved fossils. Chazy trilobites are rare but there was a metre-wide band that had more trilobites per cubic foot than anyplace I've seen.

Petosky stone found on a beach in Lake Huron, near Sarnia, Ontario. It's a rock/fossil with remnants of a "rugose coral," deposited about 350 million years ago. Named after Petosegay, 19th century Odawa merchant. State stone of Michigan. Location where I found it surprised me.

This is extremely pedantic and highlights one of my most annoying "well-actually" style traits but I can't help it; the anachronism that Rocko is set in the Great Lakes Region but all the houses have flat roofs cuz it was animated by people in southern California who never have rain or snow bugs me
I used to collect Petoskey stones when I was a kid visiting my grandparents in Michigan. Every time I see one it takes me back to Lake Michigan in the summers as a kid.

I usually ignore cement chunks with rocks because they have nothing in them, but this one had something! I thought it was another horn coral, but my uncle said it’s an ammonite! Perhaps scaphites! First ever ammonite for me. And all just a few mins away on the shores of Lake Ontario. #FossilFriday

Favosites Friday! Some info:
Favosites are a type of fossilized coral. They are roughly 400 million years old (I think).
I found them at the shore of Lake Ontario today. I am a dork.
The people sunbathing at the lake thought I was weird.

For #FossilFriday, this week’s beach haul of corals n crinoids from Lake Michigan. Featuring rugose (horn), favosites (Charlevoix stones), some teeny hexagonaria (the famous petoskey), and a halysite (chain) if you squint, and a goodly amount of crinoid beads I rotate them through my bee drinker.
Lake Michigan walk Saw lots of fossils & several fish carcasses that clearly were a delicious lunch!

‪TM today‬ ‪.bsky.social‬ 3h
#rockhound
#rockpolishing This nice fossil rock has brachiopods and some coral indents. From Door County, Wisconsin, Lake Michigan.

I also look for Petoskey and other Devonian corals. These are from
Lake Huron. The second photo is from a different period (Silurian). It’s called Michelinia.

The shores of Petoskey are shaped by time—waves smoothing stones, tides shifting the landscape, water carving its own artistry. Explore more at JoeClarkPhotographer.com #art #photography #Petoskey #GreatLakes

Found a pudding stone which isn’t as common on this part of the Great Lakes.

so this is a petoskey stone before being tumbled by lake michigan? neato. I grew up on the Great Lakes and had quite a Petoskey stone collection, but none looked like this.

Hexogonaria percarinata (colloquially Petoskey stone). I also find these in Devonian bedrock around Lake Michigan and around Port Huron (Lake Huron).

There is a name for those stones, which I don’t remember at the moment. Common along Lake Superior, and they are naturally caused, not human made.
#FossilFriday A few of my recent finds from Lake Huron, including a few brachiopods.

Love it! We have something like it around Lake Huron, in Michigan (Petoskey stones) and Ontario (different names for fossilized hexigonaria coral colonies). But your black version is so coooool. Ours are grey limestone.

The Great Lakes are a treasure trove of cool fossils that can be found along the lake shores and inland. Great family fun! #fossils #trilobites #brschiopods #crinoids #petoskeystones #coral
adventuresinnorthernmichigan.com/great-lakes-...
A fine collection of hag stones from a summer trip to the south shore of Lake Michigan

I had to look that up myself--they're basically stones formed from the "slag" (waste) of former iron-smelting factories that people collect on this part of the shore of Lake Michigan. They're pretty! northernnailpolish.com/blog/leland-...
Not really a fossil, but fulgurites are still pretty cool. Dune buggy business unearths fossilized lightning measuring over 2 feet from Lake Michigan dunes www.mlive.com/life/2025/05...

I went to the shores of Lake Michigan recently, near Milwaukee. Here are three of my favorite stones from that trip (pictured wet - they are still in Stage 1 tumbling). #rocks #rocksky #minerals #fossils
There are probably other fossilized coral rocks found on shores around the world, but Petoskey stones specifically can only be found around Lake Michigan!

A different sort of stone I see often along Lake Michigan, not any kind of fossil, but relatively bland sedimentary-type rock with “inclusions” that seem to incite a kind of “rust” or other oxidation or reaction on the surface when the stones erodes enough to reveal the inclusion. Iron inclusion?
Petoskey stone. Ancient corals. They frequently wash up on the shores of Lake Michigan. Cool very old fossils. en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Petoske...
Took a trip back in time to the Devonian Period at Bay Shore, MI today #fossils

This is the best, but I lost it in the divorce since he found it. Horn coral also found in Lake Michigan. Had to consult an old geologist boyfriend when we found it in 2016. Think it is something like a million years old. Puts some shit in perspective about time.
neat- I've never seen ocean stones like that. In the upper Midwest they're created by the strong currents in Lake Superior and the rocky lake bed (so we call them superior stones).
Beautiful Lake Superior stone necklace. Sliced and hand polished by me. #rockhound #jewelry #wetsaw #lakesuperior
I grew up in Michigan and remember looking for agates and petoskey stones on lake beaches. Lake Superior doesn’t have petoskey stones. 😆

Surprised to learn that they are found lower along Lake Huron region, where I found this. The first map shows their locations in blue. Second, same, shows the pink areas "D" for deposits from the Devonian period where these fossils are found.
The trick for me, and I’ve found quite a few small ones, is take off your sun glasses! You’d be amazed the difference it makes. I’ve also found a LOT of fossils on Lake Huron.
I found my first petosky stone on the shores of Lake Huron! Then I found another, and then I found a local who was handing them to me everytime she found one.

460’ above #LakeMichigan on the Inner Port Huron Moraine, first family trip to #SleepingBearDunes Doing our bit to support our #NationalParkService and get the girls to their first park unit! Found great limestone #MichiganErratics with rugose #fossils …but no quartz #Geology #Michigan #GreatLakes
Mid vacation fun finds. It was stormy overnight and cloudy today but fossil hunting was on point! #lakehuron #fossils Now it's time to finish reading #handmaidstale

I basically have liquid rock where I am in the U.S. Great Lakes region. Thanks Silurian inland sea coral reefs.
I found lots of those (all smaller than 4cm wide) in the Devonian shale by the great lakes when I was growing up, and this definitely looks like a bigger one. :-)

The name Petoskey comes from Ignatius Petoskey, an Odawa leader who lived during a period of enormous change in the Great Lakes region. Born in 1787, Ignatius Petoskey lived through the early years of the expansion of American settlement into Odawa homelands.
#America250 #PetoskeyHistory
I’m a fossil collector on the Great Lakes in Michigan. I just fell in love with crinoids!!
Petoskey stones are fossilized coral, and are found on the northern Michigan shore of Lake Michigan. They're the official stone of Michigan.

By far the smallest (and the cutest) stone with a natural hole I’ve found. 21 mm, smooth. I don’t wear necklaces, but would make a good pendant. Maybe I’ll put it on my keychain. Found along Lake Michigan, in Indiana. Not rare, at least on this beach. Walk for an hour or so, and
Now, if you found that Petoskey Stone outside the perimeter of Lake Michigan (say, Colorado), I'd say that was quite impressive. :)
I love this - the stone reminds me of the beautiful waters of Lake Michigan.

HELL YEAH! These are some amazing specimens! The kinds of fossils I find in the Great lakes area mostly consist of coral/plants and the occasional brachiopod, though at times they can be completely crystalized here! REALLY rare to find though I've only ever found one and that was last year :o
Makes me want to pick up Petoski stones. I miss the Great Lakes. A lot. So thanks.

Right? I had no.. context or any real thoughts about the Great Lakes at all till moving here lol. It took me until recently to re-learn them all. This beach that was made up of these smooth stones was incredible. the water was so clear, the stones just felt like ancient relics.
Looks a little like our Petosky stone around the Great Lakes.
Around the Great Lakes, we hunt for Petosky stones. Finding a large intact stone is a major win. I know your feeling, I think.
Did I show you what Nora got me to celebrate the end of the year? That's a Petoskey stone, which is a coral fossil that's only found in and around Lake Michigan.
by far the best moments of my summer—maybe my year—were spent hunting for petoskey stones in lake michigan. no thoughts, just rocks.

I began my stone tool working life in 1982, in this photo from 2003 I'm working with relatively poor grade chert from Lake Michigan beach cobbles.
My best work was using stone scrapers for brain tanning hides, a stone blade is far superior to steel.

As a rockhound myself, I applaud this worthy endeavor! My favorite hunting grounds are on Lake Superior, but have gotten great stones from that area you show, as well.
Thank you, the smaller ones are from Lake Huron and of course quartz and last last one is from anMica Mine

One of my favorite critters: Brachiopods! I grew up finding fossils of brachiopods from the Devonian era scattered on the shores of southern Lake Huron. When I was in my early 30s I finally got to see a living brachiopod in real life while scuba diving in Northern California. Sheer excitement!! 🥳

"Cozy Clam", 2025. Acrylic on stone. This bashful buddy is soaking up sun in Oscoda, MI—hidden at Three Mile Park along Lake Huron! If you spot it, scan the QR and tag us—we’ll share your pic! #Clam #oscoda #RockArt #ArtDrop #FoundArt #QRArt

Oh that's so cool. I know pudding stones can be found in (where I think) you are, but I hadn't seen a stone like yours. I found an awesome pudding stone in Lake Huron last summer, but the pebbles weren't as bright as yours. The gas pockets make sense for the colours (I think?) 🪨
I have found a couple chain coral—Devonian ((Lake Huron). Mine are not as nice as yours.
I chipped and carved a few #arrowheads for fun, using stone pieces I picked up near Lake Ontario, here in Toronto, CANADA

I assumed it had to do with the fossils found in Illinois and the Great Lakes region. A few Cenozoic and plenty of Permian fossils are available in the region
I love seeing everyone's collections! I have been collecting my coral fossils for about a decade now. I have around 150 from various areas of the Great Lakes

A geological hotspot in the Atlantic Ocean, previously located under the Great Lakes, may explain their formation. Collaborative efforts on Tetiaroa aim to enhance understanding of its unique coral ecosystem.
Good catch. Do you think you can pick up five smooth stones from one of the Great Lakes beaches?
Funny, it doesn't look confused. I think it knows it's a Petoskey stone. It knows it's fossilized coral from the Devonian period. It also knows it came from the Great Lakes, probably Michigan. 😉
If you live by the Great Lakes then you'd understand. What's your high score for skipping stones?

I was hoping, as always, to find some fossil stones at the beach. You can find them along the Great Lakes, but they seem fairly rare at least at this beach, compared with all of the other stones that are out there. Didn’t find any. Love the old bricks though that somehow make their way into the

The Northwestern corner of Michigan believe it or not! They get turned over from winter ice melt. Petoskey and Charlevoix stones are named after the towns in Michigan they are often found in. Basically a long Lake Michigan from Mackinac City to Traverse City in mid-late spring
My cousin Denise just found this fossil on the shores of Lake Michigan. Does anyone know what it is?
It's like a Petoskey stone! Which of course is fossilized coral found only in Lake Michigan.
Lake Michigan beaches in Northern Michigan are the only place in the world, aside from a few inland lakes in that region, where #Petoskey stones, the #Michigan state stone, can be found.
Thank you for these wonderful words, Kelly! So many beautiful stones and more (driftwood, fossils, etc) to find in Lake Michigan!

Yooperlite stone — found on the shores of Lake Michigan, and are UV reactive (cool!). For this pair of earrings; I have used:
- Yooperlite stone round beads
- hoop earring findings with loops, antique silver tone
- German silver wire
- sterling silver ear wires. -drop length is about 2 inches.

Is this the place you mean? That’s a good haul. Seems like the stones must be wet to look so clear? I know that Petoskey stones aren’t found “just around Petoskey,” (I found many in Lake Huron in Canada) but I didn’t know they had many of them along western shores of Lake Michigan.
A lil necklace I made for my homies birthday. With a Lake Superior stone #rockhound #wetsaw
Hearts to all! Heart shaped stones collected along the shores of Lake Superior.

This week on social media I learned about Platteville limestone, Chaska brick, and the local buildings that utilized these building materials. I’ll get the hat trick if someone posts about Lake Superior brownstone and buildings that used that colorful stone.

This unique pendant is made from a stone found on the shores of Lake Superior, and is white-gray in color. It's wire-wrapped with square sterling silver wire and is ready to hang on your favorite necklace! jrier.etsy.com/listing/1123... 🧵 1/2

What has tentacles, cosmic vibes, and a serious case of the cuteness? "Cthulhu Cutie" (2025). Acrylic on stone. Now hiding out on the Lake Huron Pier in Oscoda, MI. Find it, snap a selfie, and tag us—we’ll repost your discovery! #Cthulhu #Oscoda #RockArt #Weird
